Alright, your back, let's roll forward here with the handshake. Now you might think that a handshake is a handshake, it's just simply a handshake, but there are actually different types of handshakes and only one of them is the right one. We call the right type of handshake the standard co-operators handshake. What that is is where you meet someone's hand, head on, you're not above them, you're not below them, and you don't try to out shake them, to shake them like a rag doll, you meet the pressure equally. So one gives you a squeeze, you give them an equal squeeze. They give you a bit harder squeeze, you give them a little bit harder squeeze. Now what you don't want to do is get into a Mexican squeeze off with them, like, you know, go ahead, I'll squeeze, yeah, that's not what you want to do. But within reason, you essentially want to return pressure in kind. The key is with the co-operators handshake, you want to meet the hand out in front, it's standard, right? It's not like you're over them, not under them. You're a co-operator looking to build consensus, get into rapport, and it's a very powerful handshake. Now, remember, the worst handshake to me is that dead, limp, fish shake, hey, nice to meet you, right? Yeah, by the way, you hate when people shake the hand like that. You know what that's saying? It's actually a power move. And someone says, yeah, I'll give you that, like, I'm not even going to give, expend energy to shake your hand the right way. So much someone trying to have power over you versus the, you know, the animalistic, you know, the athletes handshake where you shake someone's hand like, nice to meet you. They pull your arm out of your socket. In fact, when I had my shoulder surgery recently, my doctor warned me, he goes, listen, be really careful, because there's some maniacs out there. Now is the word he used, maniacs, who when they shake your hand, they try to shake you up and down to prove a point. Well, now think about that in terms of a salesperson. You know, what would you think of someone who shook your hand like that? He just shook you like a rag doll. So your hand was going up and down. Would you feel like that's someone you'd want to be in business with someone you could trust? No, that person's got something to prove. Something wrong with that person. So be very careful when you shiz men and women, when you shake someone's hand, you want to use that standard cooperators handshake. You meet the equal pressure and kind, not above, not below. And that sets you up to get into report. Let's move on now to another aspect of building trust. Eye contact. This is a big one. Now I'm going to give you a number here. Ready? 72%. That's the amount of eye contact you're supposed to keep when you have a conversation with someone. You're like, 72. Ha, ha, ha. So that's the number that was tested in the lab. And they had all these different tests for people. You know, based on how much eye contact is trust built or does trust decay? And what they found is that anytime eye contact drops below that level, trust is broken. Report starts to decay. Very powerful. I'm not going to tell you a story. Growing up in my son had a friend called Lucas. Nice kid, right? Except Lucas had this one problem. Lucas hated to make eye contact. And because of that, I hated the kid. I feel bad to say it, but I, I didn't, I didn't trust the kid. I didn't like the kid. I'm like, what's wrong with that kid? Is he out to do something? Guess what? Lucas just had a problem. He was shy. He outgrew it. Lucas now makes perfect eye contact. But when he was a kid, but I say, hi, Lucas. He'd be like, hi, Mr. Belfort. I'm like, hey, I'm like, hey, I'm like, hey, I'm like, hey, I'm like, hey, I'm like, hey, I'm like dodging me, right? And I did not like this kid. Well, he was, he didn't trust him. When you don't make eye contact with someone, it breaks rapport. It breeds contempt. They say, what is this person hiding? Now, conversely, why is that number 72? Well, it's not a hundred. You can't cause you're like, how you doing? Go ahead. I gave you the blink. Go. What's wrong with, you don't want to get into a Mexican stare off with someone. Right. So again, there's, you know, you want to talk something, you break eye contact for a second, you look away. If you're thinking now, listen, I'm hoping that you're, you're at least adept enough this naturally that you don't have to sort of count percentages here. I'm giving you a guideline. Just be aware that if you're the sort of person naturally tends to avoid eye contact, you have to literally retrain your brain to make sufficient eye contact in a sale setting, or it's going to hurt you. You must maintain at least 72% eye contact to get into a rapport and maintain rapport. Otherwise you will not be trusted. All right. So just be diligent about this. And you know, if again, if you have a problem here, train yourself, practice, drill and just learn to maintain that eye contact, even if you have to like actually pick spots within a presentation where you look away strategically, like I'll do that for instance, I'll be talking, you know, and I'll say I'm talking and I, and I, and I know it's like, I shouldn't be making any more. I'll just, I'll say, anyway, I look down into some thinking and I'll break eye contact for a second and then look back so I can use eye contact to my advantage and break eye contact strategically. Hmm. Let me really think about that. Oh yeah, you're right. And I look back and then make eye contact again. So it's very easy to manage this. I just want you to be aware that if you're one of those people who tends to look down, look away, you're going to have a very tough time getting into rapport people and becoming a world class closer. So it's time to retrain your brain now. For most of you, it's going to be pretty easy. It's not a difficult thing to do, but either way, you can retrain your brain. This is an important thing.
